revision.text:
'''妙福寺'''（みょうふくじ）は、[[千葉県]][[銚子市]]妙見町にある[[日蓮宗]]の寺院。山号は海上山。境内に[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る妙見宮があることから町名の由来となり、また[[藤]]の寺としても知られる。
==歴史== この寺の創建年代については不詳であるが、元は[[八日市場市]]（現在の[[匝瑳市]]）にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、[[正和]]年間（[[1312年]]～[[1317年]]）に日蓮宗に改宗された寺である。その後、現在地に移された。
==所在地== *千葉県銚子市妙見町1465
==外部リンク== *[http://www.myokensama.jp/ 海上山妙福寺]
==関連項目== *[[藤まつり]]
[[category:千葉県の寺|みようふくし]] [[category:法華系仏教|みようふくし]] [[category:銚子市|みようふくし]] {{buddhism-stub}}

==歴史== この寺の創建年代については不詳であるが、元は[[八日市場市]]（現在の[[匝瑳市]]）にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、[[正和]]年間（[[1312年]]～[[1317年]]）に日蓮宗に改宗された寺である。その後、現在地に移された。
==所在地== *千葉県銚子市妙見町1465
==外部リンク== *[http://www.myokensama.jp/ 海上山妙福寺]
== 関連項目 == * [[藤まつり]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:みようふくし}} [[Category:銚子市の寺|みようふくし]] [[Category:法華系仏教|みようふくし]] {{buddhism-stub}}

== 歴史 == この寺の創建年代については不詳であるが、元は[[匝瑳市]][[入山崎]]にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、[[正和]]年間（[[1312年]]{{ndash}}[[1317年]]）に日蓮宗に改宗された寺である。その後、現在地に移された。

== 歴史 == この寺の創建年代については不詳であるが、元は[[匝瑳市]]入山崎にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、[[正和]]年間（[[1312年]] - [[1317年]]）に日蓮宗に改宗された寺である。その後、現在地に移された。

{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 妙福寺 |画像 = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = [[千葉県]][[銚子市]]妙見町1465 |山号 = 海上山 |寺号 = |宗旨 = |宗派 = 日蓮宗 |寺格 = |本尊 = |創建年 = 正和3年 |開基 = 日高 |開山 = 日祐 |別称 = 富士の寺 |正式名 = |札所等 = |文化財 = |地図= |公式HP= |公式HP名= }} '''妙福寺'''（みょうふくじ）は、[[千葉県]][[銚子市]]妙見町にある[[日蓮宗]]の[[寺院]]。[[山号]]は海上山。境内に[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る妙見宮があることから町名の由来となり、また[[藤]]の寺としても知られる。旧本山は、大本山[[法華経寺]]。親師法縁。
== 歴史 == 妙福寺は正和3年（1314年）4月26日、[[日蓮]]の直弟子・中老僧日高を開基とし、中山法華経寺三世の[[日祐]]によって開創された。
元は[[匝瑳市]]入山崎にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、改宗されたのち、現在地に移された。完成までに約33年の歳月を費やしている。
妙見宮本殿には、聖徳太子の作で本朝北辰像の初めての作と伝えられる北辰妙見大菩薩像が祀られている。この像は、千葉県にゆかりの深い[[源満仲]]が深く尊信したもので、代々源家に伝えられてきたものである。その後、[[源頼朝]]、[[豊臣秀吉]]も尊崇するようになったとされる。各所を経て、江戸時代に入ると、江戸城中からさらに多古城主[[松平家]]に伝わり、正徳五年になって平山久甫をはじめ、幕府および松平家などが発起人となり、当山に祀られるようになったという。
先代廣野日意上人は大本山中山法華経寺143世に栄晋した。

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]

The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]

{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myofukuji Chōshi}} [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Chōshi, Chiba]] [[Category:Chōshi history]] [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myofukuji Temple (Choshi City)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:{{subst:FULLPAGENAME}}]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>

Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]

The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>

'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.

The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>

'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|12=draft}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺|12=draft}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺|12=draft}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市|12=draft}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|12=draft}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲|12=draft}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|12=draft}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲|12=draft}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|12=draft}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺|12=draft}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺|12=draft}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|ja|匝瑳市|12=draft}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|ja|聖徳太子|12=draft}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲|12=draft}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

21 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:妙福寺 (銚子市) up to rev 90298796
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 妙福寺 |画像 = [[file:myofukuji.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = [[千葉県]][[銚子市]]妙見町1465 |山号 = 海上山 |寺号 = |宗旨 = |宗派 = 日蓮宗 |寺格 = |本尊 = |創建年 = 正和3年 |開基 = 日高 |開山 = 日祐 |別称 = 藤の寺 |正式名 = |札所等 = |文化財 = |地図= |公式HP= |公式HP名= }} '''妙福寺'''（みょうふくじ）は、[[千葉県]][[銚子市]]妙見町にある[[日蓮宗]]の[[寺院]]。[[山号]]は海上山。境内に[[妙見菩薩]]を祀る妙見宮があることから町名の由来となり、また[[藤]]の寺としても知られる。旧本山は、大本山[[法華経寺]]。親師法縁。
== 歴史 == 妙福寺は正和3年（1314年）4月26日、[[日蓮]]の直弟子・中老僧日高を開基とし、中山法華経寺三世の[[日祐]]によって開創された。
元は[[匝瑳市]]入山崎にあった[[真言宗]]寺院で、改宗されたのち、現在地に移された。完成までに約33年の歳月を費やしている。
妙見宮本殿には、聖徳太子の作で本朝北辰像の初めての作と伝えられる北辰妙見大菩薩像が祀られている。この像は、千葉県にゆかりの深い[[源満仲]]が深く尊信したもので、代々源家に伝えられてきたものである。その後、[[源頼朝]]、[[豊臣秀吉]]も尊崇するようになったとされる。各所を経て、江戸時代に入ると、江戸城中からさらに多古城主[[松平家]]に伝わり、正徳五年になって平山久甫をはじめ、幕府および松平家などが発起人となり、当山に祀られるようになったという。
先代廣野日意上人は大本山中山法華経寺143世に栄晋した。
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|山門 myofukuji2.jpg|境内 myofukuji3.jpg|境内 </gallery> == アクセス == JR総武本線ほか 銚子駅 徒歩5分。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ 海上山妙福寺]
== 関連項目 == * [[藤まつり]]
{{buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みようふくし ちようし}} [[Category:日蓮宗の寺院]] [[Category:千葉県の寺]] [[Category:銚子市の歴史]] [[Category:銚子市の建築物]]

'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]]. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]].
Originally located in [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa]], at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]]. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as [[:en:Minamoto no Mitsunaka|Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11446985]] Wikidata name: Myōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|qid=Q206611}} |image =File:Myofukuji.jpg | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Chōshi|qid=Q694317}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.727927|N|140.829926|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Q11509949|qid=Q11509949}} }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]]. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]].
Originally located in [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa]], at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]]. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as [[:en:Minamoto no Mitsunaka|Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11446985]] Wikidata name: Myōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}} |image =File:Myofukuji.jpg | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Chōshi|WD=Q694317}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.727927|N|140.829926|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Q11509949|WD=Q11509949}} }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]]. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]].
Originally located in [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa]], at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]]. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as [[:en:Minamoto no Mitsunaka|Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}
==References== {{reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11446985]] Wikidata name: Myōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}} |image =File:Myofukuji.jpg | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Chōshi|WD=Q694317}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.727927|N|140.829926|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Q11509949|WD=Q11509949}} }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]]. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]].
Originally located in [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa]], at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]]. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as [[:en:Minamoto no Mitsunaka|Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]]. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of [[:en:Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokkekyō-ji]].
Originally located in [[:en:Sōsa|Sōsa]], at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]]. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as [[:en:Minamoto no Mitsunaka|Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Myōfuku-ji (Chōshi)]][[ja:妙福寺 (銚子市)]]
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611}} |image =File:Myofukuji.jpg | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Chōshi|WD=Q694317}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.727927|N|140.829926|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Q11509949|WD=Q11509949}} }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren Shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bosatsu}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|WD=Q786113|lt=Hokkekyō-ji}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|WD=Q786113|lt=Hokkekyō-ji}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|en|Sōsa|WD=Q858367|lt=Sōsa}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōfuku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|WD=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}} |image =File:Myofukuji.jpg | location = Myōkenchō 1465, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan | mount = Kaijōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = Shōwa 3 (1314) | founder = Nikkō | main founder = Nichiyū | alias = Temple of Wisteria | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Chōshi|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.727927|N|140.829926|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Q11509949|WD=Q11509949|1=Q11509949}} }}
'''Myōfuku-ji''' (妙福寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren-shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q206611}} temple located in Myōkenchō, Chōshi, Chiba, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kaijōzan. The temple is known for enshrining {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bosatsu|1=Myōken}} in its precincts, giving the town its name, and for its famous wisteria trees. The temple’s former main temple affiliation is the Grand Head Temple {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|WD=Q786113|lt=Hokkekyō-ji|1=Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)}}. It belongs to the Oyashi lineage of Nichiren Buddhism.
== History == Myōfuku-ji was established on April 26, 1314 (Shōwa 3), by Nikkō, one of Nichiren's direct disciples, with the founding overseen by Nichiyū, the third chief priest of {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|WD=Q786113|lt=Hokkekyō-ji|1=Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)}}.
Originally located in {{ill|Sōsa|en|Sōsa|WD=Q858367|lt=Sōsa|1=Sōsa}}, at a Shingon sect temple in Iriyamasaki, it was converted to the Nichiren sect and relocated to its current site. Construction took approximately 33 years to complete.
The main hall of Myōken Shrine houses a statue of the Great North Star Myōken Bodhisattva, believed to be the first such representation in Japan, attributed to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}. The statue was deeply revered by prominent figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, who passed it down through the Minamoto clan. Later, figures like {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} and {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} also revered it. During the Edo period, it was transferred from Edo Castle to the Matsudaira family, rulers of Takō Castle. In 1715 (Shōtoku 5), it was enshrined at Myōfuku-ji through the efforts of various benefactors, including the Matsudaira family and the Tokugawa shogunate.
A prior chief abbot, Hirono Nichii Shōnin, ascended to become the 143rd chief priest of the Grand Head Temple, Hokkekyō-ji.
<gallery> Myofuku-ji (Choshi).JPG|Main Gate myofukuji2.jpg|Temple Grounds myofukuji3.jpg|Temple Grounds </gallery>
== Access == * A 5-minute walk from JR Sōbu Main Line and Chōshi Station.
== External Links == * [http://www.myokensama.jp/ Kaijōzan Myōfuku-ji Official Website]
== Related Articles == * [[Wisteria Festival]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}
